narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic performance, family background, and study plans to forecast first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, LSAT score of 33, middle-income family background, third-year decile rank of 3, and plan to attend part-time—and predicted an 83% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The three most influential factors in this decision were your part-time enrollment status, your third-year academic decile, and your family income bracket, ranked in that order of impact. Your choice of part-time study had the strongest push toward the failure prediction, as it's uncommon (only about 7% of students opt for it) and the model associates it with higher bar failure rates, unlike the vast majority who go full-time. Your third-year decile of 3, well below the typical 5.53 average, further nudged the prediction toward failure by signaling weaker performance relative to peers later in school. Your middle-income background played a smaller but still negative role, as the model links it to slightly elevated failure risk compared to higher brackets. Meanwhile, your undergraduate GPA sat right around the average of 3.21 and your first-year decile of 5 was close to typical, so they didn't sway things much either way. Overall, your profile stood out from most successful applicants, who tend to have stronger late-stage grades, full-time commitments, and different socioeconomic edges. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law—many overcome similar hurdles—but the model's data-driven lens flagged these as red flags for bar success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above the 36.7 average, strengthening your later academics through targeted prep, exploring full-time options if feasible, and applying to schools with holistic reviews that weigh your unique background more favorably. With focused improvements, you can reshape this story and secure a spot where you thrive.
